mappings.wvs_description: Perceived importance of major life domains including family, friends, leisure, politics, work, and religion. Items tap conceptually distinct domains rather than a single latent trait.
Emphasis on child qualities reflecting autonomy, creativity, and self-direction such as independence, imagination, and tolerance.
Emphasis on child qualities reflecting conformity, obedience, religious faith, and good manners.
Emphasis on child qualities reflecting thrift, perseverance, and unselfishness.
Unwillingness to have various social outgroups (e.g., immigrants, people of different race/religion, homosexuals) as neighbors, reflecting prejudice and social distance.
Unwillingness to have neighbors who engage in stigmatized behaviors such as drug use or heavy drinking.
Self-reported happiness, life satisfaction, and perceived freedom of choice/control over one's life.
Count of active memberships or participation across various voluntary organizations, reflecting civic engagement and social capital.
Count of voluntary organizations to which the respondent reports belonging (binary membership indicator), reflecting associational social capital breadth.
Endorsement of prioritizing men over women in employment when jobs are scarce, reflecting gender-based labor market attitudes.
Endorsement of prioritizing nationals over immigrants in employment when jobs are scarce, reflecting ethnocentric labor market attitudes.
Belief in the moral and social importance of work, including viewing idleness negatively, work as a societal duty, and prioritizing work over leisure.
Belief that men are superior to women in public roles (politics, business, education) and that women's employment harms children, reflecting traditional gender ideology.
Perception that a wife earning more than her husband causes relational problems, reflecting traditional gender-economic norms.
Normative beliefs about obligations within the family, including the duty to have children and to care for aging parents.
Degree of confidence in key domestic governmental, political, and public institutions in Mexico.
Degree of confidence in non-governmental, charitable, and women's organizations.
Degree of confidence in international and supranational organizations such as the UN, IMF, World Bank, WHO, WTO, NATO, and ICC.
Perceptions of the extent and actors involved in corruption, including bribery and gift-giving to obtain public services.
Perceptions of the fairness, cleanliness, and integrity of the electoral process.
Inglehart's materialist vs. post-materialist value priorities measured through paired forced-choice items.
Preference for non-democratic forms of governance including strongman rule, technocracy, military rule, and theocracy.
Ratings of how essential various democratic features are for a country's governance.
Evaluations of the desirability of democracy, its importance, and the current democratic performance in Mexico.
Engagement in conventional and unconventional offline political actions such as petitions, boycotts, demonstrations, strikes, and donations.
Engagement in political activities conducted through digital means including searching for political information, e-petitions, and organizing online.
Frequency of using various media sources to obtain political information.
Self-reported frequency of voting at local and national levels.
Beliefs about whether science and technology improve life, create opportunities, and make the world better.
Preferences along the economic left-right spectrum including income equality, state vs. private ownership, government responsibility, and competition.
Attitudes toward broad societal changes including less emphasis on work and greater emphasis on technology.
Factual knowledge questions about international organizations (UN Security Council, IMF headquarters, WHO mandate).
Acceptance of authoritarian measures such as military takeover and religious law interpretation in governance.
Latent dimension capturing core supernatural and religious beliefs including belief in God, afterlife, heaven, and hell.
Behavioral and self-identified religiosity including service attendance, self-described religiousness, and religious affiliation.
Degree to which individuals privilege their own religion and religious authority over science and other worldviews.
Moral permissiveness toward dishonest civic and economic behaviors such as claiming undeserved benefits, fare evasion, tax evasion, stealing, and bribery.
Tolerance toward behaviors related to sexuality and reproduction including homosexuality, prostitution, abortion, premarital sex, and casual sex.
Moral permissiveness toward personal life-course decisions including divorce, suicide, and euthanasia.
Degree of moral justifiability attributed to various forms of interpersonal and political violence.
Orientation on whether religion or secular reasoning should guide personal and social life, capturing the religion-science and faith-reason tension.
Degree of generalized trust toward people outside one's immediate social circle, including strangers and those of different religions or nationalities.
Belief that immigration brings beneficial outcomes such as filling jobs, strengthening cultural diversity, providing refuge, and improving lives of people from poorer countries.
Belief that immigration produces harmful consequences including increased crime, terrorism, unemployment, and social conflict.
Sense of belonging and emotional attachment across nested geographic levels from local community to the world.
Objective migration background of the respondent and their parents, indicating whether they or their parents were born in the country or are immigrants.
Frequency of experiencing deprivation of basic human needs such as food, shelter, income, and medical care.
Perceived frequency of crime, substance abuse, drug dealing, violence, and other antisocial behaviors in one's neighborhood.
Extent to which the respondent or close family members have been victims of crime in the past year.
Extent to which people in the respondent's environment adopt protective behaviors against crime such as not carrying cash, avoiding going out at night, or carrying weapons.
Degree of worry about losing employment or being unable to provide children with a good education.
Degree of worry about large-scale violent threats including war, terrorism, and civil conflict.
Degree of agreement with government surveillance measures including video monitoring, email monitoring, and data collection on residents.
Perceptions about whether most people view freedom and security as complementary or as competing values.
Perceived frequency of police/military intrusion into private life and racist conduct as threats to personal security.
Latent disposition reflecting negative or dismissive attitudes toward science and its relevance in everyday life.
